transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
It's like half red.
So I'm just not camera ready today, but I'm going to be in the next.
No problem.
problem.
That's fine.
I'm actually catching up on a late lunch too.
So if you don't mind me eating while we talk, can switch off.
No, don't worry about it.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Go ahead.
Perfect.
We can both be camera-less today.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
It's nice to finally work with you.
So you've got such a good reputation at Animals, but we never worked on the same clients or teams.
So it's nice to actually finally be able to work with you.
Yeah, yeah, same.
I remember you got laid off at the same time I did, right?
Yes, we all got the chop.
Yeah, yeah.
We all got, yeah, we got the, got chopped.
I continued like freelancing with them for like a couple of months.
And then, yeah, it was the time.
But yeah, I remember, like I remember.
Where are you during our time there?
But you were, whose team were you on?
Who was your manager?
I was Team Rawls, so Steph.
So it was me, Carrie, Eddie Shum, and Sydney, actually, funnily enough.
Ah, okay, nice.
So you were all, okay, I was, when I joined, I was with Kaylee, and then she very quickly went on maternity leave.
And then I was on Team Octopus with Aideen and Natalie.
Ah, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, that's nice.
Yeah, yeah.
So welcome to GrowthX.
How are you finding it so far?
So far, so good.
The first week has just been, you know, you know how it is, like the onboarding material, watching videos.
And then now I'm actually really beginning to get into like using the tools like Atlas.
Jessalyn's going to be out of town, so I'm going to be covering her clients.
So I'm to start jumping and shooting for her from Wednesday to Friday.
But apart from that, I don't have.
Any clients are a pod yet, so I'm still kind of just like learning the ropes, wanting to find out about strategy sprint and how all the workflows and pipelines and clients all work in sync.
So I really appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today.
Yeah, yeah, no, sure.
Okay, so I am on the strategy sprint team.
And to give you a little bit of just history of how, like, I joined in December, and when I joined, GrowthX was a company that was like, I think, 18 people in total.
Okay.
And we were the first managing editors who were hired.
So at the time, the company had so-called account managers who are now content managers.
And they were generating the content.
They were doing everything.
It was very scrappy.
And it was just, you know, the focus was just on delivering, like, a large volume of content.
And then the managing editors were brought in to kind of edit and kind of improve the quality.
And then a few months after that, once the company started getting more attention, we raised our Series A.
We started hiring managing directors, and they're kind of more in charge of, they're in charge of more accounts.
They're kind of leading more like the strategic part, you know, of the engagement, like suggesting additional, like work streams.
Like, you know, if they're doing just blog slash editorial, they might suggest something programmatic.
Or if a client just has questions about anything SEO related or content related, they can provide guidance.
So what my managing director was Jacob.
And then at the time I was doing, I was in charge of four or five clients.
So the bulk of my work really was editing.
At the time, we didn't have a lot of really good content managers.
Since then, this has changed.
And we've hired a lot of good content managers that are, you know, significantly making the lives of managing editors easier.
And then I got moved to the Strategy Sprints team, which is the team that when a client signs with us, they spend eight weeks with us and we build their strategy.
So this is mostly like their blog content strategy.
We create their first couple of pieces.
It's going to be, it's usually around 10 to 15 blogs that they'll get from us.
We kind of calibrate on the voice and tone and just to make sure that like we have everything set up well.
And at the end of the eight weeks, the client decides if they're going to continue with us or not.
And we might also decide that we don't want to continue working with them if it turns out that it's just not a good fit.
And then they, the client is, if they decide to continue.
And
They transitioned to an execution team, which is the pods with the managing directors, the managing editors, and the CMs.
So do you have any questions about that?
Is that, like, kind of information that was helpful to you?
Yeah, definitely.
It makes sense.
Pretty straightforward.
And then how does that work align with, like, the managing editors?
How do they sync and align with the whole strategy sprint?
What role do they play?
Yeah, yeah.
So the strategy sprint team has two managing editors right now.
We're hoping to get a third one, and I don't know if that's going to be you.
I'm not sure yet.
I think Andy's going to let you know or someone else.
But as a managing editor, like, on the team you are, if you are on the strategy sprint team, I can, like...

It really depends on the capacity and if you are, for example, if you have a lot of editing that you're doing and you're just completely swamped with editing and working with the CMS or doing keyword research, or if you're trying to improve the pipeline for a specific client, I might just take over the strategy for a new client that comes in.
Because there are two kickoffs every week, and pretty much every week there's a new set of clients who needs a strategy built.

So sometimes the managing editor will help out with that, so I might provide some input, I might provide my ideas, and then the managing editor can kind of take, we usually take a topic cluster approach to content strategies.
So I might provide, like, the initial, like, you know, draft, and then you can take that and just build upon it, like, you can do, like, so.

Spot checking in SEMrush or like just doing additional key like keyword gap analysis to see what's in there but the bulk of the work of the managing editor is once we start creating content for a client which happens between week three and four of their engagement in the strategy sprints you have to you work with a CM you edit their content and you cannot you also can join the calls with the clients to discuss their feedback on the blog that we sent over so week over week you're editing the content you're you know making sure that all the clients feedback is implemented if there's any additional notes around product messaging that the artifacts are updated and that like the CM has all the context that they need to create content in the way that the client wants it and there's also just like you know working with the CM them
To help them, like, improve and, you know, if there's anything that, where they have a skill gap, you can kind of show them, you know, help them out.
But yeah, I think the bulk of the, in the strategy sprints, the bulk of the work is like, okay, you can set up this client with like a strategy in collaboration with me.
And then it's just like editing the content, doing additional keyword research as needed.
In the delivery, like in the execution team, which are the teams that have like a managing director, and they are the ones who get a client out of the sprints.
So this is where you will get like a fully fledged out strategy.
And you will have like a content calendar to execute against, but, so you will continue delivering content for the client, publishing, performance tracking, attending.
Weekly Syncs, reporting on the performance, just like the typical like content management stuff.
And then it's really, once you notice, you know, there's, you know, we try to like create a backlog of content.
So we always keep an eye on like approved topics.
If we're running low on approved topics, then we'll do another round of keyword research for the clusters that we already have.
If you notice that like you're running low, it depends on the industry, on the client.
If you're running low on ideas, or if some of the clusters are just, there's no more room there, like we've exhausted all the options.
Then you can suggest additional angles or additional clusters in collaboration with your director.
So your director is there very much to like support the overall strategy.
can brainstorm things with them.
I know that you have like strategy experience, and it's not something.
I encourage the writing of thelyncast.
That's, like, foreign to you.
So if you want to have more agency there, feel free.
I don't think, you know, any director will mind that.
So, yeah, I think that's, you know, it also, like, depends on your clients and what their needs are.
So you will see, like, once you start working in a team, you will kind of be able to figure out, like, okay, what are the specific needs for this client?
How much, you know, input are they expecting from us?
Do they just want us to execute?
And, yeah, I think that that's kind of, but the editing of the content, that's probably, I would say, maybe 60% of the work.
It depends on your CMs.
So, like, editing and just, like, refining the pipelines, that's kind of a big part of the managing editor role.
Okay, that makes sense.
about But you let

Since you've practically been with growthx.ai from the beginning, from managing editor to now editorial account strategist, you experienced the highs and the lows, the challenges and the growth pains and people come and go.

What would you say for me coming in as a new person, what advice or tips would you give me to be able to succeed in this role or any shortcuts or anything along that line that you think would help me do my job as a manager as well?
Yeah, I think people that do really well here are people who kind of, if they have a problem in terms of like, how do I say this?
Like, if you are, if you have, like, I'll give you an example of Vivek, for example.
When we, he was, because he joined, he was the same, he joined at the same time as me.
And we, at the time.

We didn't have a reporting setup, so all the reporting was very manual, it was very time-consuming, and Vivek literally built a Looker dashboard that we could all replicate and just fill in with data for our own clients, and it saved us so much time.

So, you know, if there's, if you have noticed that there's a problem or there's an area for improvement, and you know, you have an idea for how to fix it, you are just more than welcome to just, like, talk to your director and be like, hey, like, I'm gonna try this, and I'll see, you know, I'll report back and see what happens.
So that's, like, you just have, like, if you have agency, you are very welcome to just try different things and see what works.
And I think, obviously, if you do have an issue, and you don't know how to solve it, then, you know, definitely ask for help, but I think the people that do...
Really well here are just people that have just that sense of like, let me, let me see what works.
Let me play around and try to solve this problem.
Yeah.
And I think also just like people who have high standards in terms of just like, it's, I think we have a lot of MEs who have very high content quality standards.
Um, and I think that kind of drives us to constantly push the dev team to do better and like create better workflows.

Um, I think once you start working with the content, um, obviously like it's not perfect, but I will tell you when I first joined, we were using Aerofs and the quality of the output back then was way, way below.
So it is right now and like, it's hasn't even been a year.
So, and we've only been using Atlas for like four.

So there's been major improvements in a very short period of time and that's been because the delivery team, especially the content managers, the managing editors, we've been very, like I say, annoying, but we have been like bringing up these issues that we're having and like the content production process, like what's taking us a lot of time and they have been working to kind of improve it over time.
So I think just having like just agency drive to kind of ask questions and suggest solutions and I think that that really works.

And also I think like you are welcome to like once you start working with CMs, and this is more applicable if you're on the execution team, not a strategy sprint team, but if you're working with like a specific CM, you can build a process with them that kind of fits your workflow.
So for example, you might
Give them like an editing checklist that they have to use when they're creating content.
Or, you know, you might have them follow a specific template for specific styles of articles.
Like you have a lot of room to just come up with ideas for your clients and execute against them.
You don't have to wait for someone else to like sign off on it really.
Like you, I think Dave, you know, the leadership team has hired a lot of really well qualified content people.
in the managing editor position and they trust the managing editors to do a good job and to have good ideas for how to execute on a content strategy.
I think from a cultural perspective, this is very much a startup and it's just very fast moving.
And I don't think we have like a culture beyond that.
So it's very much just like doing the work and it's, you know, there's not a...
I think it will take some time to kind of develop that part of the company.
So basically just stay curious, always trying to find ways to improve my work and just the work overall and the quality overall, my clients and the team.
Yeah, and also I think developing good relationships with clients, I think that's very helpful.

I think because we see our clients on a weekly basis, there is just a big opportunity there to build trust with them and just make sure that, you know, they see us as experts and advisors so that they feel comfortable.
Like if they're thinking about like improving their website or launching something new on their site, they ask us, hey, do you think this is a good idea?
Do you, you know, have you done this in the past?
And also like making suggestions to them, like on their blog, if you've noticed, if you noticed that they don't have like...
Call-to-action buttons on their blog that are very good.
You can let them know.
So just kind of like building that trust with them so that they start seeing you as a partner.
I think because customer retention is so important to the company right now, I think that's something that's an important part of customer management is just like building that relationship.
Okay.
That all makes sense.
I'm taking notes.
Other than that, don't really have any other questions.
I just wanted to get a lay of the land in terms of like strategy sprint and get advice from your perspective in terms of like how to be a good ME.
And if you have any other tips, I would really appreciate it as well.
But I really don't have any other questions.
I'm just absorbing everything right now, taking calls with as many people as I can.
Yeah.
Yeah, for sure.
And I mean, listen, like, don't, you know, You're...
You're...
You'll pick things up as you go.
A lot of the knowledge here is institutional.
So we are working on improving like documentation and processes.
But, you know, there are some things that, you know, other people will just have to tell you how to do it or show you how to do it.
So if you run into something and you're not sure, like, how we do it or, you know, whatever, just feel free to ask.
But yeah, I think, I'm not sure, but I think you'll be on the sprint team.
I cannot tell you for sure.
But yeah, the sprint team is pretty, like, we do need an ME right now.
Like we're an ME short.
So the sprint team consists of me, Sydney, Natalie, and then a couple of other CMs.
And there's Andy, obviously, and Eric, who's another account strategist.
But wherever you end up.
Like, you'll, you know, it's the, the, the point is the same, like, just make the clients happy.

And, you know, like, if there are issues with, you know, the workflows to let us know, for example, we have a client on the sprint team right now, hyper exponential, and the managing editor has been kind of struggling with the, like the output, and it's just not, it's just not, you know, hitting the marks.
So we've been able, like, we've been brainstorming ways to improve it.
But for, you know, we just in the end concluded, we need the dev team to build an agentic pipeline for this client, because the content is just too complex.
And I think it's very important, like, for the ME to communicate, like, hey, this really isn't working.
Like, I cannot, you know, realistically, I cannot spend three hours editing a piece of content for this client is just taking too much time.
And we can be like, okay, yeah, like, let's, let's work with.
The dev team to improve that because, you know, if she were to just like not say anything, she'd probably be overworked and like really struggling and we would never know.

So it's really important that like don't take if the workflow isn't doing what it's supposed to be doing and you've tried different options, you've asked for help, like you've tried all the tweaks and it's just still not working.

Then like flag it to the dev team so that they can work on improving it because like I think it helps to treat Atlas as a product and if it's not doing what it's supposed to be doing, then that's a problem for the platform.
So, you know, just make sure to like bring up issues and nobody's going to be like, you know, no one's going to think that you don't know what you're doing.
Sometimes it's just a matter of like getting the right people involved and adding the right tweaks.
That makes sense.
I really.
I your time.
don't have any other questions.
was just like a session for me.
I really appreciate you taking the time, especially since you're sick at the moment.
No, no problem.
I'm happy to help.
In the meantime, if there's anything else you have, you know, any questions around or anything else you need help with, you know, just send me a message.
I will do.
Okay.
All right.
Amazing.
Thanks, Will.
No problem, Aida.
Enjoy the rest of, well, I think it's evening you're in.
Yes, yes, yes.
Yeah, yeah.
All right.
I'll see you.
Bye.
Take care.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboarding catch-up between new hire Will Ruzvidzo and experienced team member Aida Knežević to discuss GrowthX.ai's structure, processes, and tips for success.](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=130.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX.ai's rapid growth: 18 people in Dec '22 → Series A → expanded team structure (MDs, MEs, CMs)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=136.0)
  - [Strategy Sprint team: 8-week client onboarding, blog strategy creation, 10-15 initial posts](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [ME role: 60% editing, also strategy input, client management, process improvement](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=323.0)
  - [Success factors: proactive problem-solving, high standards, client relationship building](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)

## Topics

### Company Evolution and Structure

  - [Dec '22: 18 people, first MEs hired to improve content quality](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=136.0)
  - [Post-Series A: Added Managing Directors (MDs) for strategic leadership](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)
  - [Current structure: Strategy Sprint team (8-week onboarding) → Execution teams (ongoing content delivery)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [Roles: Managing Directors (MDs), Managing Editors (MEs), Content Managers (CMs)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)

### Strategy Sprint Process

  - [8-week client onboarding period](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [Develop blog content strategy (typically topic cluster approach)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=394.0)
  - [Create 10-15 initial blog posts](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [Calibrate voice, tone, and setup](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [Client decides to continue or not; GrowthX.ai also evaluates fit](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)

### Managing Editor Responsibilities

  - [Editing (≈60% of workload)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=637.0)
  - [Collaborate on strategy development](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=394.0)
  - [Keyword research and topic ideation](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=549.0)
  - [Client call participation and feedback implementation](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [CM mentoring and skill gap support](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [Content calendar management and performance tracking](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=508.0)

### Tips for Success at GrowthX.ai

  - [Proactively identify and solve problems (e.g., Vivek's Looker dashboard)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=691.0)
  - [Maintain high content quality standards](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=789.0)
  - [Exercise agency in suggesting improvements and solutions](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=691.0)
  - [Customize workflows with CMs to fit your style](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=880.0)
  - [Develop strong client relationships as a trusted advisor](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=980.0)
  - [Communicate issues, especially with content production workflows](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=1140.0)

### Company Culture and Growth

  - [Fast-paced startup environment](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=942.0)
  - [Continuous improvement in tools and processes (e.g., Aerofs → Atlas)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=815.0)
  - [Leadership trusts MEs to execute and innovate on content strategies](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=922.0)
  - [Company culture still developing beyond work focus](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=942.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Will to continue onboarding and absorb information from various team members](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=1068.0)
  - [Potential placement on Strategy Sprint team (to be confirmed)](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=1107.0)
  - [Will to maintain proactive approach in identifying areas for improvement](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=691.0)
  - [Aida available for any follow-up questions or support as needed](https://fathom.video/share/zRQtUYBGDzf3ysx5rvx_DvuWUgMyjLLF?tab=summary&timestamp=1269.0)
